abstract:
This brief provides an updated synthesis of peer-reviewed research and fiscal data regarding Teach For America (TFA), an organization that fast-tracks recent college graduates into two-year teaching commitments in high-needs urban and rural schools. Since its inception in 1990, TFA has experienced nearly 2,000% growth, fueled by hundreds of millions of dollars in public and private funding. Despite this expansion and significant media acclaim, the evidentiary basis for TFA’s effectiveness remains a subject of intense academic debate.

A review of recent literature, including a prominent 2013 Mathematica study and updated peer-reviewed analysis, suggests that the impact of TFA corps members on student achievement is highly contextual and generally modest. While secondary-level TFA math teachers occasionally outperform their peers, novice TFA teachers in other subjects and grade levels often produce lower learning gains in reading and mathematics compared to fully credentialed beginning teachers. Furthermore, the program faces a systemic challenge regarding retention: longitudinal data indicates that more than 80% of TFA teachers leave their initial placements after three years, whereas approximately 50% of non-TFA teachers remain in the profession after five years.

The report also examines the substantial economic burden associated with this high-turnover model. When accounting for recruitment fees, federal AmeriCorps stipends, and the recurring costs of replacing departing teachers, the total societal cost of a two-year TFA commitment can exceed $70,000 per recruit—roughly $6 million more per 100 teachers than hiring traditionally prepared staff. Given that TFA placements represent only 0.002% of the national teacher workforce and offer mixed academic results, the authors argue that the program functions as a costly and temporary "Band-Aid" rather than a sustainable solution to educational inequity. The brief concludes with recommendations for districts to prioritize evidence-based reforms with larger proven effect sizes, such as Pre-K investment and class-size reduction, and provides a research template for citizens to evaluate TFA’s local impact.
